questions.question: Which statement best defines computer forensics?
options: Preventing every computer-based attack before it occurs
Identifying, preserving, examining and presenting digital evidence using legally acceptable methods
Encrypting sensitive files during an investigation
questions.description: Computer forensics involves the scientific handling and analysis of digital evidence while preserving its integrity and legal acceptability.

questions.question: An employee accesses a restricted organisational folder without permission, but the incident is currently treated only as an internal policy violation. How should it be classified at this stage?
questions.description: Unauthorised activity violates permission or organisational policy. It becomes a computer crime when the conduct also satisfies the applicable criminal law.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of cyber laws during a forensic investigation?
options: To determine the strongest hashing algorithm
To regulate legal authority, evidence collection and admissibility
To identify the operating system automatically
To eliminate the need for chain of custody
questions.description: Cyber laws define the legal limits within which digital evidence may be searched, seized, collected and presented.

questions.question: Which sequence represents a valid six-stage computer-forensics process?
options: Analysis -> Collection -> Preservation -> Identification -> Presentation -> Examination
Identification -> Preservation -> Collection -> Examination -> Analysis -> Presentation
Preservation -> Presentation -> Identification -> Analysis -> Collection -> Examination
Collection -> Deletion -> Analysis -> Encryption -> Reporting -> Disposal
questions.description: A commonly used six-stage model is identification, preservation, collection, examination, analysis and presentation.

questions.question: A web server is attacked and its stored files are modified. What role does the server primarily play in this incident?
questions.description: The server is the target because the attacker directly compromised and modified it.

questions.question: An investigator receives a laptop as potential evidence. What should be done before beginning technical examination?
options: Document, label and preserve the device
Allow the owner to verify its contents
questions.description: Evidence must first be documented and preserved so that its original condition and handling history are recorded.

questions.question: Which forensic activity protects evidence from alteration before detailed analysis?
questions.description: Preservation protects the original evidence and prevents contamination, modification or destruction.

questions.question: What is the principal goal of forensic analysis?
options: To produce the largest possible evidence image
To reconstruct relevant events and determine what occurred
questions.description: Forensic analysis attempts to reconstruct events, correlate artifacts and determine facts objectively.

questions.question: Which document defines the actions, roles and communication procedures to be followed after an incident?
questions.description: An incident-response plan establishes responsibilities, procedures, escalation paths and communication requirements.

questions.question: What does chain of custody primarily establish?
options: The encryption algorithm used on evidence
Continuous accountability for evidence possession and handling
The operating system used by the investigator
questions.description: Chain of custody records who handled evidence, when it was transferred and why it was accessed.

questions.question: During an evidence transfer, the receiver signs the form, but the sender's identity and transfer time are missing. What is the main problem?
questions.description: Missing transfer details create a gap in evidence accountability and may weaken admissibility.

questions.question: During an incident-response simulation, malware is detected on a production system. What should the responder do first?
options: Follow the approved response plan and document the system state
Informally copy selected files to a personal drive
questions.description: The responder should follow authorised procedures and document actions before making changes that may destroy evidence.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of an evidence checkout log?
options: To record who accessed evidence, when and for what purpose
questions.description: The checkout log supports accountability by documenting evidence issue, examination and return.

questions.question: What is the first responder's primary responsibility at a digital crime scene?
options: Secure the scene and prevent evidence alteration
questions.description: The first responder must protect the scene and preserve potentially relevant evidence.

questions.question: What is forensic duplication?
options: Creating multiple filenames for one file
Creating an accurate forensic copy of the evidence source
Compressing evidence to reduce its size
Restoring files from the Recycle Bin
questions.description: Forensic duplication creates a verified copy that can be examined without working directly on the original evidence.

questions.question: Which method is most appropriate for authenticating a forensic duplicate?
questions.description: Matching cryptographic hashes provide strong evidence that the source and forensic copy contain identical data.

questions.question: Which field is essential in an evidence checkout log?
options: Checkout and return date and time
questions.description: The date and time of checkout and return establish when the evidence was under an examiner's control.

questions.question: An examiner connects the original disk directly to a normal workstation and begins browsing files. What is the main forensic mistake?
options: The workstation has too much storage
The original evidence may be modified
questions.description: Directly accessing original media without suitable protection may modify metadata or other evidence.

questions.question: What is the main objective of an initial incident assessment?
options: Determine incident scope, severity and potential evidence sources
questions.description: Initial assessment determines what happened, what systems may be affected and how the response should proceed.

questions.question: What is the principal function of an incident-notification checklist?
options: To ensure that required parties are notified consistently
questions.description: A notification checklist ensures that management, technical teams, legal personnel and other relevant parties are contacted.

questions.question: What is the decimal value of hexadecimal 0x10?
questions.description: Hexadecimal 10 represents 1 x 16 + 0, which equals decimal 16.

questions.question: How many hexadecimal digits are required to represent one byte?
questions.description: One hexadecimal digit represents four bits, so two hexadecimal digits represent eight bits or one byte.

questions.question: The hexadecimal bytes 41 42 43 are interpreted using ASCII. What text do they represent?
questions.description: ASCII hexadecimal 41, 42 and 43 represent A, B and C.

questions.question: What is the decimal equivalent of hexadecimal offset 0x20?
questions.description: Hexadecimal 20 equals 2 x 16, which is decimal 32.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes encoding from encryption?
options: Encoding always requires a secret key
Encryption protects confidentiality, while encoding changes data representation
Encoding provides stronger confidentiality than encryption
questions.description: Encoding changes representation for compatibility, whereas encryption uses a key to protect confidentiality.

questions.question: What does a hex editor primarily allow an examiner to view?
options: Raw bytes and their character representation
questions.description: A hex editor presents raw file or disk bytes, usually alongside an ASCII or character view.

questions.question: Which property is expected from a cryptographic hash function?
options: Fixed-length output for a given algorithm
Recovery of the original file from the hash
Use of the same value for similar files
questions.description: A hash algorithm produces a fixed-length digest regardless of the input size.

questions.question: What is a hash collision?
options: Two identical inputs producing different hashes
Two different inputs producing the same hash
questions.description: A collision occurs when distinct inputs generate the same hash value.

questions.question: A file is converted to Base64 and can be restored without using a secret key. Which operation was performed?
questions.description: Base64 is an encoding method and does not provide cryptographic confidentiality.

questions.question: An examiner calculates a file hash, changes one byte and calculates the hash again. What should normally occur?
options: The new hash should be identical
The new hash should be significantly different
The hash should become the filename
The original file should be reconstructed
questions.description: A small input change normally causes a substantially different cryptographic digest.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a forensic Standard Operating Procedure?
options: Allow every examiner to use an unrelated method
Ensure consistent, documented and repeatable handling
Eliminate the requirement for legal authority
questions.description: SOPs provide standardised procedures that support consistency, reliability and accountability.

questions.question: What should be documented when processing a powered-on Windows computer?
options: Screen state, running condition, connections, date and time
Only the number of desktop icons
questions.description: The system's visible and physical state must be recorded before significant actions are taken.

questions.question: Why should an investigator avoid unnecessarily interacting with a live system?
options: Interaction may alter volatile and non-volatile evidence
Interaction always destroys the hard disk
questions.description: Commands and tools used on a live system may change memory, logs, timestamps and other artifacts.

questions.question: Which practice best supports repeatability during crime-scene processing?
options: Following an approved SOP and recording every action
Allowing multiple users to handle evidence without records
Analysing evidence before assigning a case number
questions.description: Approved procedures and complete documentation allow another examiner to understand and reproduce the process.

questions.question: A powered-off suspect disk must be examined. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Boot the suspect operating system normally
Connect through a write blocker and create a forensic image
Install analysis software on the disk
Copy only visible documents using File Explorer
questions.description: A write blocker helps protect the original media while a forensic image is acquired.

questions.question: A running Windows system may contain an unlocked encrypted volume. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Immediately remove power in every case
Document the state and perform authorised live collection before shutdown
Allow the user to continue working
questions.description: An active encrypted volume may become inaccessible after shutdown, so authorised live collection may be necessary.

questions.question: What is an important forensic implication of using a tool on a live system?
options: The tool may change the system being examined
The tool automatically makes evidence admissible
The tool eliminates all privacy concerns
The tool cannot create new artifacts
questions.description: Running a tool consumes memory, creates processes and may alter files, logs or network state.

questions.question: What does forensic laboratory accreditation primarily demonstrate?
options: That every conclusion is automatically correct
That the laboratory follows recognised competence and quality requirements
That evidence no longer requires authentication
That investigators may exceed legal scope
questions.description: Accreditation assesses whether a laboratory follows established technical and quality-management standards.

questions.question: Which privacy principle requires investigators to examine only data relevant to the authorised investigation?
questions.description: Data minimisation limits collection and examination to information necessary for the authorised purpose.

questions.question: Which characteristic is essential when performing a forensic investigation?
options: Presuming that the suspect is guilty
questions.description: Investigators must remain impartial and base conclusions on verified evidence.

questions.question: During an authorised examination, an investigator finds unrelated confidential medical records. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Restrict access and avoid examining them beyond the authorised scope
Delete the records from the evidence
Send them to another investigator informally
questions.description: Unrelated private information must be protected and handled according to legal scope and privacy requirements.

questions.question: A new forensic tool has not been validated by the laboratory. What should the examiner do?
options: Use its output as the sole basis for conclusions
Validate the tool or corroborate its output using an approved method
Hide the tool name from the report
Modify the evidence until the tool works
questions.description: Unvalidated tool results should not be relied upon without testing and independent corroboration.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Sysinternals Suite in forensic work?
options: Examining Windows processes, sessions, handles and system activity
questions.description: Sysinternals contains utilities for inspecting Windows processes, logons, network connections, autoruns and system resources.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes FTK from FTK Imager?
options: FTK is a full forensic-analysis platform, while FTK Imager focuses on acquisition and evidence preview
FTK Imager is only a network scanner
Both tools are exclusively mobile-forensics tools
questions.description: FTK provides broader case processing and analysis, while FTK Imager is commonly used for imaging, previewing and exporting evidence.

questions.question: What is OSForensics primarily used for?
options: Searching, indexing and analysing operating-system artifacts
questions.description: OSForensics supports file search, indexing, deleted-file analysis and examination of system artifacts.

questions.question: Which tool is most suitable for directly viewing and interpreting raw hexadecimal bytes?
questions.description: A hex editor displays and allows examination of raw byte values.

questions.question: An examiner must create a forensic disk image and verify its hash. Which tool is most directly suitable?
questions.description: FTK Imager can acquire physical or logical media and perform image verification.

questions.question: An examiner needs to identify suspicious programs configured to start automatically on Windows. Which Sysinternals tool is most appropriate?
questions.description: Autoruns displays programs, services, drivers and other components configured for automatic execution.

questions.question: What is active-state forensic collection?
options: Analysis performed only after a system has been destroyed
Collection of artifacts while a system is powered on and operating
Recovery of data exclusively from optical media
questions.description: Active-state collection gathers information from a running system before it changes or disappears.

questions.question: Which artifact is most likely to be lost when a computer is powered off?
questions.description: Running processes and other memory-resident artifacts are volatile and may disappear when power is removed.

questions.question: Which group contains only active-state artifacts?
options: Running processes, open files and network connections
Device label, invoice and evidence bag
Disk model, case number and examiner name
questions.description: Processes, open files and network connections reflect the current state of the running system.

questions.question: Why must every live-collection command be documented?
options: Commands do not affect the system
Live collection changes the system and must remain accountable
Commands are legally confidential in every case
questions.description: Running commands changes memory and other artifacts, so the examiner must record the resulting collection footprint.

questions.question: Which Windows command displays network connections together with numerical addresses and owning process identifiers?
questions.description: netstat -ano displays connections, listening ports, numerical addresses and associated PIDs.

questions.question: Which command is most suitable for listing currently running Windows processes?
questions.description: tasklist displays currently running processes and their process identifiers.

questions.question: Which Linux location commonly contains system and service log files?
questions.description: Linux commonly stores operating-system, authentication and service logs under /var/log.

questions.question: What forensic information is commonly available through the Linux /proc file system?
options: Information about running processes and kernel state
questions.description: /proc is a virtual file system containing process and system-state information.

questions.question: What is mobile forensics primarily concerned with?
options: Identifying, preserving, acquiring and analysing evidence from mobile devices
questions.description: Mobile forensics applies forensic principles to smartphones, tablets, SIM cards and related storage.

questions.question: Which item is a common source of evidence in mobile forensics?
questions.description: Mobile devices may contain call history, messages, contacts, application data, media and location information.

questions.question: Which Linux command lists currently running processes?
questions.description: The ps command displays information about active processes.

questions.question: Which Linux command is most suitable for identifying files currently opened by processes?
questions.description: lsof, meaning list open files, correlates processes with files, sockets and other open resources.
